q: Your BigQuery table has 5 years of transaction data. Analysts query only the last 30 days. The table currently costs $8,000/month in query costs. What is the single most impactful optimization?
opts: Enable BigQuery BI Engine for sub-second queries by reserving in-memory capacity over the full 5-year table, accelerating aggregations without changing the underlying scan cost
Partition the table on transaction_date and ensure queries include a date filter — BigQuery will prune irrelevant partitions
Cluster the table on customer_id to speed up individual customer queries via block-level pruning, while still scanning the full 5 years of column data on every analyst query
Move data older than 30 days to Cloud Storage Nearline and rebuild the table as a BigQuery external table pointing to GCS, expecting BigQuery to skip cold objects automatically
explain: Partitioning on transaction_date means queries with WHERE transaction_date >= DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL 30 DAY) scan only 30 of 1,825 partitions — drastically reducing bytes scanned and cost.
concept: BigQuery partitioning, partition pruning, cost optimization

q: You need a database for a global e-commerce platform: strong consistency, ACID transactions, SQL queries, horizontal scaling, and 99.999% availability across 3 continents. Which database is the only GCP-native option meeting ALL requirements?
opts: Cloud SQL with multi-region read replicas backed by HA failover and PostgreSQL logical replication for cross-continent reads, accepting eventual consistency on the replica side
Cloud Spanner with multi-region configuration
Firestore in Native mode with multi-region replication providing strong consistency for single-document reads but lacking full SQL JOIN support across collections at scale
BigQuery with ACID transactions via DML and multi-statement transactions, paired with the Storage Write API for transactional inserts at the analytical warehouse layer
explain: Cloud Spanner uniquely provides externally consistent ACID transactions, SQL support, horizontal scaling without re-sharding, and 99.999% SLA with multi-region configurations.
concept: Cloud Spanner, global consistency, horizontal scaling, ACID transactions

q: A financial application stores time-series account balances in Bigtable. Row keys are formatted as account_id#timestamp. Over time, performance degrades as recent data overwhelms older data with hotspot patterns. What is the root cause and fix?
opts: Timestamps as the trailing part of row keys cause monotonically increasing keys, routing all new writes to the same tablet. Fix: reverse the timestamp or use a hashed prefix
Bigtable automatically handles time-series workloads via its internal tablet splitting algorithm — performance degradation is almost always caused by underlying SSD hardware aging on the cluster
The column family count is too high; consolidate down to a single column family with a unified GC policy so Bigtable can compact SSTables more aggressively across the cluster
Increase Bigtable node count to distribute tablet pressure linearly, since Bigtable automatically rebalances hotspots across additional nodes once they are provisioned in the cluster
explain: Sequential/monotonically increasing row keys create write hotspots — all new writes go to the last tablet. Reversing the timestamp scatters recent writes across all tablets.
concept: Bigtable row key design, hotspot prevention, reversed timestamps

q: You need to choose a Cloud Storage storage class for a disaster recovery archive that must be retained for 7 years but will only be accessed if a disaster occurs (< once per year). What class minimizes total cost?
opts: Standard — highest availability for critical DR data with no minimum storage duration, but the highest per-GB storage rate making it cost-prohibitive at 7-year retention scale
Nearline — for data accessed less than once per month with a 30-day minimum storage duration and moderate retrieval fees, designed for warm backup workloads
Coldline — for data accessed less than once per quarter with a 90-day minimum storage duration, priced between Nearline and Archive for quarterly DR rehearsals
Archive — for data accessed less than once per year with 365-day minimum storage duration
explain: Cloud Storage Archive class is designed for data accessed fewer than once per year with the lowest storage cost ($0.0012/GB/month) — perfect for DR archives held for 7 years.
concept: Cloud Storage storage classes, Archive, cost-access tradeoffs

q: Which BigQuery feature allows you to query data in Cloud Storage (Parquet, ORC, CSV) without loading it into BigQuery native storage?
opts: BigQuery Materialized Views over Cloud Storage objects, which precompute aggregations from GCS-resident Parquet files and refresh incrementally as new files land in the bucket prefix
BigQuery External Tables (using CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE or federated queries)
BigQuery Data Transfer Service with a Cloud Storage source connector, which loads GCS files into native BigQuery storage on a recurring schedule using managed extraction jobs
BigQuery Omni for multi-cloud queries, designed to run BigQuery SQL against data residing in AWS S3 or Azure Blob Storage via Anthos-managed compute in those clouds
explain: BigQuery External Tables allow SQL queries directly against data in Cloud Storage without ingestion. Supported formats include Parquet, ORC, CSV, Avro, JSON.
concept: BigQuery External Tables, federated queries, Cloud Storage formats

q: Your Firestore data model has a collection of 10 million 'orders' documents. A query filters on customer_id and then orders by created_at. What must you create to support this query efficiently?
opts: A single-field index on customer_id only, which Firestore creates automatically and which is sufficient for any combined equality-and-ordering query across multiple fields
A composite index on (customer_id ASC, created_at ASC) — Firestore requires composite indexes for multi-field queries
No index needed; Firestore auto-indexes all fields and builds composite indexes on demand the first time a multi-field query pattern is observed by the query planner
A Collection Group index on all subcollections so the query planner can scan across nested order subcollections under each customer document for ordered range retrieval
explain: Firestore automatically creates single-field indexes. However, queries that filter on one field AND order/filter on another require a composite index — without it, Firestore returns an error.
concept: Firestore composite indexes, query limitations, index management

q: You are designing a BigQuery table for storing user events. The table receives 100K inserts/second via the Storage Write API. Which combination of settings ensures both ingest performance and query performance?
opts: Partition by ingestion time (_PARTITIONTIME), cluster by user_id and event_type, use committed mode streams in Storage Write API
Use a single unpartitioned table with no clustering and rely on the Storage Write API's default pending mode to coalesce writes into a single optimized columnar block per second
Partition by user_id (string hash); the Storage Write API will auto-balance writes across hashed partitions and BigQuery will prune by user prefix at query time automatically
Enable materialized views over the raw stream — they replace the need for partitioning by precomputing user/event aggregations and serving them with sub-second query latency
explain: Ingestion-time partitioning auto-assigns rows to date partitions. Clustering on user_id and event_type enables efficient block elimination. Committed mode Storage Write API provides exactly-once guarantees.
concept: BigQuery ingestion-time partitioning, clustering, Storage Write API

q: A company needs to encrypt BigQuery data with their own encryption key stored in an on-premises HSM. Which solution supports this?
opts: Default Google-managed encryption keys (GMEK), which encrypt all BigQuery data at rest transparently using Google-controlled KMS keys with no customer-side key custody
Customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) using Cloud KMS with keys hosted in a Google-managed software keyring, optionally backed by Cloud HSM for FIPS 140-2 Level 3 compliance
Customer-supplied encryption keys (CSEK) via Cloud External Key Manager (Cloud EKM) pointing to on-premises HSM
AES-256 encryption applied at the application layer before insertion, with the application managing all key derivation locally and storing ciphertext as BYTES columns in BigQuery
explain: Cloud External Key Manager (Cloud EKM) allows GCP services to use encryption keys stored in third-party key management systems like on-premises HSMs. CSEK with Cloud EKM is the mechanism for using HSM-resident keys with BigQuery.
concept: CMEK, CSEK, Cloud EKM, HSM integration, BigQuery encryption

q: What is the maximum number of columns (fields) supported in a single BigQuery table?
opts: Unlimited
explain: BigQuery supports up to 10,000 columns per table. Consider using nested STRUCT or ARRAY fields to group related columns in wide-table designs.
concept: BigQuery limits, schema design, nested/repeated fields

q: You want to ensure that a BigQuery dataset is only accessible from within your VPC and not from the public internet. Which control enforces this?
opts: Set IAM bindings to domain-restricted sharing for your organization, which enforces an organization policy preventing principals outside the domain from being granted bigquery.dataViewer
Enable Private Google Access on the subnet and configure VPC Service Controls with an access level for the BigQuery API
Use a BigQuery authorized view to limit access by exposing only filtered rows and columns to consumers, while leaving the underlying dataset reachable from the public BigQuery API endpoint
Enable BigQuery Column-Level Security on all sensitive columns via Data Catalog policy tags, requiring datacatalog.fineGrainedReader on the taxonomy for any cross-network query access
explain: VPC Service Controls creates a security perimeter. Adding BigQuery to the perimeter and configuring access levels to allow only traffic from your VPC via Private Google Access ensures BigQuery is inaccessible from the public internet.
concept: VPC Service Controls, Private Google Access, network-based access control

q: What is the purpose of BigQuery's INFORMATION_SCHEMA and when would a data engineer use it?
opts: It stores the actual data in BigQuery tables in an optimized columnar format and is the underlying physical storage layer queried whenever a SELECT runs against a logical table
It provides metadata views (TABLES, COLUMNS, JOBS, PARTITIONS, etc.) used for governance, auditing, and capacity planning
It is the UI layer for BigQuery Studio that powers the schema explorer panel, autocomplete suggestions, and the query history view shown in the Cloud Console
It contains GCP billing data for BigQuery usage, exposing per-project slot consumption and per-query bytes-billed values streamed from Cloud Billing export tables
explain: INFORMATION_SCHEMA is a set of read-only views exposing BigQuery metadata: table schemas, job history, partition info, slot usage. Data engineers use it to audit slow queries, monitor data freshness, and optimize reservations.
concept: BigQuery INFORMATION_SCHEMA, metadata queries, operational monitoring

q: Cloud Bigtable is being used to store IoT sensor readings (10M devices, 1 reading/second each). The SLA requires p99 read latency < 10ms. The cluster has 10 nodes and CPU is at 80%. What should you do?
opts: Switch from Bigtable to Cloud Spanner for better read latency, since Spanner's TrueTime-backed reads can serve single-row lookups from a local zonal replica with sub-10ms tail latency at IoT scale
Add more Bigtable nodes — Bigtable performance scales linearly with node count
Enable Bigtable compression on column families to reduce on-disk I/O and improve read latency, since smaller SSTables mean fewer disk seeks per ReadRows call across the cluster
Increase the column family count and store more data per row to reduce round trips, since a single ReadRows can return all related cells in one RPC and drop p99 latency
explain: Bigtable's performance scales linearly with node count. At 80% CPU utilization with latency SLA risk, adding nodes is the correct action. Bigtable nodes can be added with no downtime.
concept: Bigtable scaling, node count vs. performance, linear scalability

q: You need to store machine learning model artifacts, training data, and prediction results, with different teams needing different access levels. Which Cloud Storage feature manages access at the folder/prefix level?
opts: Cloud Storage Object Lifecycle Management rules with prefix-based filters, which transition objects between storage classes per team but do not enforce read/write permissions at the prefix level
Cloud Storage Uniform Bucket-Level Access with IAM Conditions on object names/prefixes
Cloud Storage Object ACLs for per-object access, manually applied to every artifact and training file so that each team has explicit reader or writer entries on its own objects
Cloud Storage HMAC keys for programmatic access control, where each team is issued an HMAC key scoped to a specific prefix of the bucket for signed request authentication
explain: Best practice is Uniform Bucket-Level Access with IAM Conditions. IAM Conditions on resource.name allow prefix-based policies (e.g., grant data-scientists access to gs://bucket/training-data/* only).
concept: Cloud Storage IAM, Uniform Bucket-Level Access, IAM Conditions

q: A data team wants to use BigQuery's time travel feature. A table was accidentally DROPped 4 days ago. Can they recover it?
opts: No — dropped tables cannot be recovered through time travel; the FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF clause only operates on tables that currently exist in the dataset metadata catalog
Yes — BigQuery time travel retains table data for up to 7 days; use FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF to recover data. Dropped tables can be recovered with bq cp
No — time travel only applies to data-level changes from DML statements like INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE, and is not retained for DDL operations such as DROP TABLE or TRUNCATE
Yes — but only if a BigQuery table snapshot was manually created with CREATE SNAPSHOT TABLE before the drop event, since snapshots are the only durable recovery primitive
explain: BigQuery's time travel window is 7 days by default. For a table dropped 4 days ago, query its data using FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF with a timestamp before the drop. The bq cp command can restore a dropped table within the retention window.
concept: BigQuery time travel, table recovery, FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF

q: You need to share a BigQuery dataset with an external partner without giving them direct table access. The partner needs to query a subset of columns and rows. What is the recommended approach?
opts: Create an authorized view that filters columns and rows; grant the partner access to the view only in a separate dataset
Export the filtered data to a Cloud Storage bucket as Parquet files on a recurring schedule, then share the bucket with the partner's service account using IAM Conditions on the prefix
Enable BigQuery Data Transfer Service to push a filtered copy of the data to the partner's project on a daily schedule, replicating the subset of columns and rows they need
Grant the partner's service account roles/bigquery.dataViewer on the full table and rely on a row-level access policy plus column-level policy tags to restrict their visible subset
explain: Authorized views are the standard mechanism for row/column-level security. Create a view with SELECT on specific columns and WHERE filters, authorize it against the source dataset, then share only the view dataset with the external partner.
concept: BigQuery authorized views, row/column security, cross-project sharing

q: You have a Bigtable table with 500 billion rows. You need to perform a full table scan for a batch analytics job. Which Bigtable feature makes this efficient?
opts: Bigtable does not support efficient full table scans for analytics; instead, export the table to BigQuery via the federated query connector and run all batch analytics from there
Use Bigtable's batch ReadRows API with row range scans; or export to Cloud Storage via Dataflow using the BigtableIO source for parallel scans
Enable Bigtable replication to a separate analytics-dedicated cluster and use single-cluster routing on an app profile so the analytics scan does not contend with serving traffic
Use Cloud Spanner federated queries against the Bigtable table by configuring an external data source pointing to the Bigtable instance and issuing a SQL SELECT over the rows
explain: Bigtable supports efficient range scans via ReadRows. For batch analytics, use Dataflow's BigtableIO source which parallelizes the scan across multiple workers, each reading a different key range. This provides linear-scale throughput.
concept: Bigtable full table scan, BigtableIO, Dataflow parallel scan

q: A Cloud Storage bucket has 100 TB of data across millions of objects. You need to automatically delete objects older than 90 days in a specific prefix. How do you configure this?
opts: Write a Cloud Function that runs daily on a Cloud Scheduler trigger, lists all objects under the prefix, checks creation time against a 90-day threshold, and deletes matches via the GCS API
Configure a Cloud Storage Object Lifecycle Management rule: condition age=90 days + matches_prefix='old-data/'; action=Delete — GCP applies this automatically
Use gsutil rm with a date filter inside a Cloud Scheduler job that invokes a Cloud Run service to enumerate the prefix nightly and delete objects whose ctime is older than 90 days
Use Cloud Storage Retention Policies which enforce a minimum 90-day retention floor and automatically delete objects at the maximum retention boundary after the period expires
explain: Cloud Storage Lifecycle Management applies rules automatically. A rule with condition {age: 90, matchesPrefix: ['old-data/']} and action {type: Delete} runs continuously — GCP evaluates all objects against the rules without any manual scheduling.
concept: Cloud Storage Lifecycle Management, automatic deletion, prefix-based rules

q: What is the difference between Bigtable's 'column family' and 'column qualifier'?
opts: Column family and column qualifier are functionally identical concepts in Bigtable's storage model and are used interchangeably in the API, with both defined at table creation time

Column family is a top-level grouping defined at table creation time (with its own GC policy); column qualifier is the actual column name within a family — a table has few families but potentially millions of unique qualifiers
Column family determines the row key format and sort order on disk, while column qualifier determines the encoded data type (string, int, double) of values inside each cell of the table
Column families are restricted to storing string and BYTES values while column qualifiers can only hold numeric types such as int64 and double, with type enforcement at write time
explain: Bigtable's schema: row key identifies the row; column families (defined at table creation, typically 1-5) group related columns with shared GC policies; column qualifiers are the actual column names within a family (can be dynamic, created on write). Data is stored as {row key, column family, column qualifier, timestamp} → value.
concept: Bigtable schema design, column families, column qualifiers, GC policies

q: You need to store semi-structured documents (user profiles with varying attributes) that require real-time single-document reads/writes and support for array-contains queries. Which GCP service is most appropriate?
opts: BigQuery — supports a native JSON type for semi-structured data and JSON_VALUE extraction, but query latency starts in the hundreds of milliseconds and is unsuitable for per-request reads
Firestore — document model with ARRAY_CONTAINS queries, real-time updates, and sub-millisecond reads
Cloud Bigtable — wide-column store with flexible schemas where each user profile is one row and attributes are dynamic column qualifiers within a single column family per row
Cloud Spanner — relational with JSON column support, providing SQL JSON_VALUE access and array-contains semantics through UNNEST, but optimized for transactional rather than document workloads
explain: Firestore is purpose-built for semi-structured documents with real-time updates (onSnapshot listeners), sub-millisecond reads, and rich query support including ARRAY_CONTAINS. It scales to millions of concurrent connections automatically.
concept: Firestore, document storage, ARRAY_CONTAINS, real-time updates

q: A BigQuery table has 10 columns partitioned by date. A query uses WHERE transaction_date = '2024-01-15' AND UPPER(customer_name) = 'ALICE'. How much of the table does BigQuery scan?
opts: Only the rows matching both predicates — BigQuery uses internal column-level zone maps to evaluate UPPER(customer_name) against block statistics and reads only matching rows from disk
One day's partition (transaction_date filter prunes partitions); within that partition, all rows are scanned since UPPER(customer_name) cannot use an index
Two days of partitions are read — BigQuery scans the target partition plus the adjacent calendar day as a safety buffer to account for late-arriving data in time-partitioned tables
Zero rows — the partition filter alone is sufficient for BigQuery's planner to determine that no matching data exists and short-circuit the query before scanning the partition contents
explain: BigQuery prunes partitions based on the transaction_date filter — only one day's data is read. Within that partition, BigQuery scans all rows (no secondary indexes) and then applies the UPPER(customer_name) = 'ALICE' filter. This is a key difference from traditional databases: BigQuery has no row-level indexes.
concept: BigQuery partition pruning, no row-level indexes, scan behavior

q: Which Cloud Spanner feature allows you to add new rows to an existing table without downtime and without locking existing rows?
opts: Cloud Spanner DDL ALTER TABLE statements run as offline operations requiring a maintenance window, during which the affected table is locked against writes until the schema change is fully applied
Cloud Spanner supports online schema changes — DDL statements are applied asynchronously without locking reads/writes on existing data; rows get NULL for new columns until backfilled
Cloud Spanner requires creating a brand new table with the target schema, then migrating existing data via a Dataflow job and switching application traffic to the new table after backfill completes
Use Cloud Spanner database branching to apply schema changes on a branch and validate them against a staging traffic copy before atomically promoting the branch to the primary database
explain: Cloud Spanner's online schema changes apply DDL (ADD COLUMN, CREATE INDEX, etc.) asynchronously in the background while the database continues serving traffic. New columns default to NULL for existing rows. No downtime, no row locks.
concept: Cloud Spanner online schema changes, DDL, zero-downtime migrations

q: You need to design Bigtable row keys for a multi-tenant SaaS application. Each tenant has millions of records. Which row key design prevents hotspots while enabling efficient per-tenant queries?
opts: tenant_id#record_id — groups all records for a tenant under a shared prefix and enables efficient per-tenant range scans without hashing or salting the leading bytes of the row key
Hashed tenant_id prefix (first 4 bytes of MD5(tenant_id))#record_id — distributes writes across tablets while allowing per-tenant range scans on a secondary index
Sequential auto-increment IDs as the row key — simple monotonically increasing keys that funnel all new writes onto the last tablet and create a sustained write hotspot in the cluster
Store each tenant in a separate Bigtable table — one table per tenant — and route queries to the correct table at the application layer based on the authenticated tenant's identifier
explain: tenant_id#record_id is actually correct for per-tenant queries (scans all rows with prefix tenant_id#). Hotspots occur when a SINGLE tenant generates all writes. If writes are spread across many tenants, tenant_id prefix is appropriate. Salting is needed only for single-tenant hotspots.
concept: Bigtable multi-tenant row key design, prefix scans, hotspot analysis

q: What is Cloud Storage's 'dual-region' configuration and when would you use it?
opts: Dual-region stores two copies of each object in the same zone for higher durability against disk failures, with both replicas served from a single zonal frontend for low intra-region latency

Dual-region replicates data across two specific GCP regions with turbo replication option for < 15-minute RPO; use it for disaster recovery requiring geographic separation but predictable data location
Dual-region is identical in behavior to multi-region but limited to exactly two underlying regions, with the same eventual consistency semantics and no guaranteed turbo replication RPO
Dual-region is a cost-optimization tier that halves storage costs by splitting object data into two halves across two regions, requiring both regions online to reconstruct each object
explain: Cloud Storage dual-region provides georedundancy across two specific, paired regions (e.g., NAM4 = Iowa + South Carolina). Turbo replication guarantees < 15 minute RPO. Use dual-region when you need DR redundancy with predictable data location (vs. multi-region which uses multiple unpredictable regions).
concept: Cloud Storage dual-region, turbo replication, RPO, geographic redundancy

q: You need to implement a BigQuery table that automatically expires partitions older than 90 days to control storage costs. How do you configure this?
opts: Write a Cloud Function that runs daily and issues DELETE statements against the partitioned column for any partition older than 90 days, triggered by Cloud Scheduler at midnight UTC
Set require_partition_filter=TRUE on the table and manually issue a DROP PARTITION DDL statement for each calendar day older than 90 days, scheduled through a BigQuery scheduled query
Configure partition expiration when creating the table: CREATE TABLE ... PARTITION BY DATE(event_time) OPTIONS(partition_expiration_days=90)
Use Cloud Storage Lifecycle Management to expire the underlying BigQuery partition files after 90 days by attaching a lifecycle rule to the dataset's managed storage bucket
explain: BigQuery supports partition-level expiration natively. When creating a table, set partition_expiration_days in the OPTIONS clause. BigQuery automatically deletes partitions older than 90 days — no external automation needed.
concept: BigQuery partition expiration, automatic data lifecycle, storage cost control

q: A Bigtable table stores click events with row key = user_id#reverse_timestamp. A Dataflow pipeline needs to read the last 10 clicks for each user for real-time processing. What is the efficient read pattern?
opts: Issue a full table scan via ReadRows across all row ranges, then apply a per-user top-10 sort transform inside the Dataflow pipeline using a GroupByKey on user_id with a LIMIT 10
For each user, read the first 10 rows from the row range [user_id#, user_id$) — due to reverse timestamps, the first 10 rows are the most recent 10 clicks
Read all rows for user_id by issuing an unbounded prefix scan, then perform an in-memory sort by timestamp inside the Dataflow worker before emitting the top 10 most recent events
Use a Bigtable secondary index defined on the timestamp column to look up the last 10 events per user, leveraging the index's internal ordering to skip the bulk of the row range scan
explain: With reversed timestamps, the most recent events sort lexicographically first within the user's key prefix. A row range scan [user_id#, user_id$) with a row limit of 10 returns the 10 most recent events in a single RPC — O(1) regardless of total event count.
concept: Bigtable prefix scan, reversed timestamp row key, efficient reads

q: BigQuery supports two types of external tables: regular external tables and BigLake tables. What is the key difference?
opts: BigLake tables support only the Parquet columnar format while regular external tables support every supported format (Parquet, ORC, Avro, CSV, JSON) including delimited text files

BigLake tables support fine-grained access control (row/column policies), Cloud DLP integration, and data sharing across projects. Regular external tables bypass access policies — BigLake adds a centralized security and governance layer
Regular external tables execute faster than BigLake tables for ad-hoc queries because BigLake adds a policy evaluation layer that imposes per-query overhead during predicate planning
BigLake tables require BigQuery Enterprise Edition reservations while regular external tables work with any edition including on-demand billing, with no policy enforcement at the storage layer
explain: BigLake extends external tables with centralized governance: row access policies, column masking (DLP integration), and project-independent access control. Regular external tables have no row/column security. BigLake tables can be shared via Analytics Hub with consistent policy enforcement.
concept: BigLake vs external tables, governance, access control, DLP integration

q: You need to migrate a 200 TB Cloud Spanner database to a different Spanner instance in a different region. What is the recommended approach?
opts: Use Cloud Spanner database branching to create a branch in the target region, then promote the branch atomically once cutover validation is complete on the application side
Export the Spanner database to Cloud Storage using the Dataflow Spanner export template; import to the target instance using the Spanner import template
Use Cloud Spanner cross-region replication to continuously mirror the database to the target region and then change the primary instance pointer once replicas catch up
Use Datastream for Spanner-to-Spanner change data capture migration, streaming row-level changes from the source instance into the destination instance with at-least-once delivery
explain: Cloud Spanner export/import: use the managed Dataflow template 'Cloud Spanner to GCS Avro' to export to Cloud Storage, then 'GCS Avro to Cloud Spanner' to import. For large databases, schedule during off-peak; the export captures a consistent snapshot.
concept: Cloud Spanner export/import, Dataflow Spanner templates, cross-region migration

q: A team needs BigQuery tables to be accessible to Looker but inaccessible to direct SQL clients. What mechanism restricts direct BigQuery SQL access?
opts: BigQuery cannot distinguish between Looker traffic and traffic from direct SQL clients such as the bq CLI at the API authentication layer; all requests appear identical to the IAM resolver
Use BigQuery OAuth scopes to restrict which client applications can obtain access tokens for the dataset, binding scope hashes to Looker's service account credential issuance pipeline
IAM conditions in BigQuery cannot reference the client application name or user agent string, so this kind of client-type restriction is not expressible directly through the conditions language
This cannot be accomplished with BigQuery IAM alone; you would need VPC Service Controls access levels that allowlist Looker's IP ranges while blocking other clients
explain: BigQuery IAM grants/denies access to resources but cannot distinguish client applications. VPC Service Controls access levels can restrict access based on IP ranges or device attributes — allowlist Looker's cloud infrastructure IPs while blocking direct client access. This is the closest mechanism available.
concept: VPC Service Controls, access levels, BigQuery client restriction

q: Which Cloud Spanner feature provides an automatically managed, low-latency read-only replica in the same region as your application?
opts: Cloud Spanner read replicas must be explicitly configured per zone via the CREATE INSTANCE CONFIG DDL statement, with replication topology defined manually at instance setup time

Cloud Spanner read-only replicas are automatic — every Spanner database has read-only replicas in each zone of the configured region. Use read-only transactions or stale reads to route to local replicas
Configure Cloud Spanner with a multi-region instance configuration to enable read replicas, since single-region Spanner instances serve all reads from a single zonal voting replica
Deploy a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL read replica alongside the Spanner instance to absorb read traffic, configured via logical replication from the Spanner change streams API
explain: Cloud Spanner automatically maintains replicas in each zone of the configured region. To use them efficiently, use read-only transactions (no locking) or bounded staleness reads (stale_read) which can be served from the nearest replica without requiring a global quorum.
concept: Cloud Spanner read replicas, read-only transactions, stale reads, local serving

q: What BigQuery feature allows you to create a virtual dataset in your project that points to an authorized dataset in another project, without copying data?
opts: BigQuery Authorized Views created in your project that issue SELECT statements against the remote project's dataset, requiring per-view authorization on each underlying source table
BigQuery dataset links (linked datasets) created by Analytics Hub — subscribers get a virtual dataset in their project that mirrors the publisher's data in real-time without copying
BigQuery Cross-Project Queries using the fully qualified project.dataset.table syntax, which requires the querying principal to hold dataViewer directly on the source project's dataset
BigQuery Data Transfer Service configured with a BigQuery source connector that incrementally copies the source dataset into the destination project's dataset on a recurring schedule
explain: Analytics Hub linked datasets: the publisher creates a listing; the subscriber adds a linked dataset to their project. The linked dataset appears as a regular BigQuery dataset but reads directly from the publisher's data. No data copy, no storage cost for subscriber.
concept: Analytics Hub, linked datasets, cross-project data sharing, no-copy

q: A Bigtable table experiences high read latency after adding a new application that issues many small reads. The cluster CPU is low (20%). What is the likely cause and fix?
opts: Low CPU utilization at 20% indicates the cluster is undersized for the read workload, so add more nodes to spread the read load across additional tablet servers and reduce per-RPC latency

The new application is reading many individual cells, causing high RPC overhead. Fix: redesign reads to use row range scans (read multiple rows per RPC) or batch point reads using ReadRows with multiple RowSet entries instead of one-RPC-per-row
Enable Bigtable column-family compression to reduce I/O per read and lower the latency of each individual RPC, since smaller block sizes translate directly into faster ReadRow responses
Bigtable replication between clusters is causing read-write conflicts that increase tail latency on point reads, so disable replication on the affected app profile to restore baseline performance
explain: Low CPU + high latency = network RPC overhead, not compute limitation. Many individual single-row reads generate excessive RPCs. Batching: use a single ReadRows call with a RowSet containing multiple row keys, or redesign to scan ranges rather than point lookups.
concept: Bigtable read optimization, batch reads, RPC overhead, RowSet

q: You need to store JSON configuration documents that are read thousands of times per second by microservices. Documents update infrequently (once per hour). Which storage option provides the best read latency and throughput?
opts: Cloud SQL with a JSON column queried by the microservices on every incoming request, relying on the Cloud SQL Auth Proxy and a PgBouncer connection pool to absorb the read concurrency
Firestore — sub-millisecond document reads with automatic scaling to millions of reads per second, built-in caching, and global distribution suitable for per-request configuration lookups
Cloud Storage — read the JSON configuration file directly from a regional bucket on each microservice request, accepting 10-100ms object-read latency in exchange for low storage cost
BigQuery external tables defined over a Cloud Storage JSON file, accessed via federated SQL queries from each microservice each time a configuration value lookup is needed at runtime
explain: Firestore provides sub-millisecond document reads at unlimited scale. For configuration documents read thousands of times per second, Firestore is ideal: built-in caching, automatic global distribution, and no connection management. Cloud Storage reads have 10-100ms latency — too slow for per-request configuration reads.
concept: Firestore, high-throughput reads, configuration storage, sub-millisecond latency

q: A BigQuery dataset uses column-level security with policy tags. An analyst queries a table and gets 'Access Denied' on specific columns. How do you grant them access without removing security?
opts: Remove the policy tag from those columns so they are no longer subject to the taxonomy-level access check, which makes the columns universally readable to anyone with dataset-level dataViewer
Grant the analyst the roles/datacatalog.categoryFineGrainedReader role on the specific policy tag (taxonomy node) — this grants access to tagged columns without changing the underlying policy
Add the analyst to the dataset's IAM with the roles/bigquery.dataViewer role, since dataset-level dataViewer overrides any column-level taxonomy restrictions defined via Data Catalog tags
Build a BigQuery authorized view that explicitly selects the restricted columns and share the view with the analyst, bypassing the policy tag check at the underlying source table level
explain: Column-level security with policy tags: access to tagged columns requires roles/datacatalog.categoryFineGrainedReader on the specific tag. Grant this role to the analyst on the taxonomy node for the specific policy tag. This is additive — other users without this role still have column access denied.
concept: BigQuery column-level security, policy tags, Fine-Grained Reader, taxonomy

q: You need to run a SQL query in BigQuery that must complete in under 10 seconds and return results from a 50 TB table. The table is partitioned by date and clustered by region. The query filters by date range and region. What do you verify?
opts: Check that the BigQuery project has sufficient on-demand bytes-billed quota available to scan the entire 50 TB table within the daily project-level cap configured by the billing administrator

Verify the query includes both the date filter (enabling partition pruning) and the region filter (enabling cluster block elimination); these filters ensure BigQuery scans a small fraction of the 50 TB
Enable BigQuery BI Engine on the table for in-memory column acceleration by reserving 250 GB of BI Engine capacity in the dataset's region to absorb the working set at query time
Create a BigQuery materialized view that pre-aggregates the commonly queried date and region combinations so the query reads from the materialized view rather than the underlying 50 TB table
explain: For this 50 TB table, the query must include both the partition key (date) in the WHERE clause for partition pruning AND the clustering key (region) for block elimination. Both filters together reduce the scan to a tiny fraction of 50 TB, enabling sub-10-second execution.
concept: BigQuery partition pruning + cluster block elimination, query optimization, large tables

q: What is Cloud Storage's default consistency model?
opts: Eventual consistency — reads may return stale data for a short window after a write while replicas converge across the bucket's region, with no read-after-write guarantee for new objects
Strong consistency — read-after-write is immediately consistent for all operations including list operations after object creation, deletion, or metadata updates
Monotonic read consistency only — guarantees you see your own writes once observed but does not guarantee that writers in different sessions see each other's writes in real time
Session consistency — consistency is guaranteed within a single HTTP session against the same Cloud Storage endpoint but not across sessions or across different client connections
explain: Cloud Storage provides strong consistency for all operations: after a successful write, the new object is immediately visible to all readers (including list operations). This changed from eventual consistency in November 2020. No read-your-own-writes lag exists.
concept: Cloud Storage consistency model, strong consistency, read-after-write

q: A Bigtable cluster is performing poorly on random reads. Key analysis shows reads are uniformly distributed across the key space. CPU is at 70%. What is the recommended optimization?
opts: Redesign the row key for better access locality so random reads can be coalesced into prefix range scans, reducing the per-RPC overhead and improving p99 read latency across the cluster
Add more Bigtable nodes — random reads with 70% CPU indicates compute saturation; linear scaling with nodes applies
Enable Bigtable caching by deploying a Cloud Memorystore Redis cluster in front of Bigtable with a write-through pattern, absorbing point lookups before they reach the Bigtable tablet servers
Compress all column families using the snappy compression codec to reduce on-disk I/O per read, with the expectation that smaller SSTables will translate directly into lower random-read latency
explain: Uniformly distributed random reads with 70% CPU means the cluster is near saturation. Bigtable performance scales linearly with node count — adding nodes directly increases read throughput and reduces latency. This is the correct action when key design is already optimal.
concept: Bigtable linear scaling, random read performance, node count

q: You need to implement a BigQuery table where analysts can only see their own department's data (10 departments, 10 million rows each). Which implementation is most maintainable at scale?
opts: Create 10 separate BigQuery tables, one per department, and grant each department's analyst group dataViewer on only their corresponding table within the shared dataset
Create 10 BigQuery authorized views, each with a department-specific WHERE clause filter, and grant each department's group access only to the view that filters to their department
Use BigQuery Row Access Policies (CREATE ROW ACCESS POLICY) with SESSION_USER() or IAM group binding per department; one table, automatic row filtering
Use column-level encryption per department by encrypting each row's payload with a department-specific KMS key, then distributing decryption permissions to each department's analyst group
explain: Row Access Policies scale elegantly: CREATE ROW ACCESS POLICY emea_policy ON table GRANT TO GROUP 'emea-analysts@company.com' FILTER USING (department = 'EMEA'). Create one policy per department. All 10 departments share one table; each analyst automatically sees only their rows.
concept: BigQuery Row Access Policies, row-level security, multi-tenant data

q: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL is running at 100% disk I/O. The database handles 10,000 writes/second. What is the most effective scaling action?
opts: Add more CPU cores to the Cloud SQL instance by upgrading the machine type, expecting that additional vCPU capacity will reduce the disk I/O bottleneck on write-heavy workloads
Increase the storage size — Cloud SQL on SSD automatically provisions more IOPS as storage size increases; or upgrade to a larger instance with more IOPS
Enable Cloud SQL read replicas in additional zones to offload disk I/O from the primary instance, expecting writes to be distributed across the primary plus the new read replicas
Enable Cloud SQL's automatic storage increase feature so that storage grows automatically with usage; this raises the storage ceiling but does not affect the underlying provisioned IOPS
explain: Cloud SQL IOPS scale with storage size on SSD: larger storage = more IOPS (3,000 IOPS per 1 TB minimum). If I/O is the bottleneck, increase storage provisioning to get more IOPS. Also consider upgrading instance tier for higher IOPS limits or enabling Cloud SQL Enterprise Plus.
concept: Cloud SQL scaling, IOPS, storage-based IOPS scaling

q: You need to archive 500 TB of BigQuery data to Cloud Storage for long-term retention (10 years) at minimum cost. Data may be queried occasionally. What is the optimal approach?
opts: Keep all of the data in active BigQuery storage and rely on the automatic long-term storage pricing tier, which halves the per-GB rate after 90 days of no table modifications
Export BigQuery data to Cloud Storage Archive class as Parquet files; create BigQuery external tables for occasional queries; delete from BigQuery native storage
Export the 500 TB to Cloud Storage Coldline class as compressed Avro files and accept that the data will be unqueryable from BigQuery and only readable by direct GCS object download
Keep the data in BigQuery Standard Edition and rely on BigQuery's automatic archival tier that moves inactive table partitions to Cloud Storage Archive after 90 days of inactivity
explain: Cloud Storage Archive class at $0.0012/GB/month vs BigQuery native storage long-term at $0.01/GB/month — 8x cheaper. Export as Parquet to preserve queryability via BigQuery external tables when needed. This reduces storage cost by 8x while maintaining SQL access.
concept: BigQuery archival, Cloud Storage Archive vs BigQuery long-term pricing, external tables

q: A multi-region BigQuery dataset spans US and EU. A query is run by a user in Asia. Where does BigQuery execute the query?
opts: BigQuery routes the query to the physically nearest region (Asia) by spinning up ephemeral query compute in the user's nearest available BigQuery edge region before returning results
BigQuery executes the query in the region where the dataset is stored — for US multi-region, computation happens in a US region; the query result is returned to the user regardless of their location
BigQuery replicates the dataset to Asia automatically on first query from an Asian user, caching a regional copy of the table for subsequent queries from the same user's geographic region
Query routing is user-configurable via the --location flag on the bq CLI, which causes BigQuery to materialize the dataset in the chosen location and execute the query there at runtime
explain: BigQuery compute is co-located with data storage. A query against a US multi-region dataset runs in a US region. Data never moves — results are returned to the user over the network. This is why data residency requirements must be set at dataset creation.
concept: BigQuery compute-storage co-location, query execution location, data residency

q: What is the maximum number of partitions a BigQuery table can have?
opts: 365 (one per day for one year)
Unlimited
explain: BigQuery supports up to 10,000 partitions per table (for date/timestamp partitioned tables). For integer range partitioning, the maximum is also 10,000 partitions. Design partition granularity accordingly — daily partitioning supports ~27 years of data within this limit.
concept: BigQuery partition limits, table design, partition count

q: You need to enable Bigtable's automated backups for a production table. What are the backup characteristics?
opts: Bigtable backups must be manually triggered through the Cloud Bigtable Admin API each time, and there is no native scheduled backup mechanism — third-party tooling is required to automate the cadence
Bigtable supports scheduled automated backups. You configure a backup schedule on a cluster; backups are stored within the same Bigtable instance and can be restored to create a new table
Bigtable backups are full table copies that are automatically exported to a Cloud Storage bucket in the same region as the source cluster, with one backup object created per column family
Bigtable backups replicate the source data to a different Cloud project entirely, providing project-level isolation for disaster recovery scenarios where the source project is compromised
explain: Bigtable scheduled backups: configure a BackupSchedule on a cluster (e.g., daily at 2 AM). Backups are stored within the Bigtable service (not Cloud Storage). Backup retention is configurable (up to 90 days). Restore creates a new Bigtable table from the backup.
concept: Bigtable automated backups, backup schedules, restore

q: Which BigQuery feature provides sub-second query performance on dashboards by caching results in memory on dedicated, reserved VMs?
opts: BigQuery Materialized Views, which store pre-computed aggregation results on disk and incrementally refresh as the base table changes, accelerating dashboard queries that match the view's SQL
BigQuery BI Engine — an in-memory analysis service that accelerates Looker and Looker Studio queries without changing SQL syntax
BigQuery Reservations configured with the highest priority slot pool and reserved capacity for the BI workload, which guarantees slot availability for dashboard queries during peak hours
BigQuery query cache, which retains identical query results for 24 hours and serves them from cache for any subsequent query with the exact same SQL hash and referenced table generations
explain: BigQuery BI Engine reserves in-memory capacity (1-250 GB) and accelerates SQL queries for BI tools. Eligible queries (simple aggregations, filters on cached columns) return in milliseconds. Unlike the query cache (exact query match), BI Engine caches column data and accelerates diverse queries on the same table.
concept: BigQuery BI Engine, in-memory acceleration, sub-second BI queries

q: You need to store 1 petabyte of data in BigQuery. What is the most cost-effective storage approach assuming most data is never queried?
opts: Keep all of the data in active BigQuery storage at the standard per-GB rate since BigQuery natively supports petabyte-scale tables and applies no surcharge for table size beyond active rates
Use BigQuery's active vs long-term pricing: data not modified for 90+ consecutive days automatically moves to long-term pricing ($0.01/GB vs $0.02/GB/month), saving 50%
Export the entire petabyte to Cloud Storage Archive class and rebuild all BigQuery objects as external tables, eliminating BigQuery managed storage entirely in favor of cheaper GCS rates
Use BigQuery Reservations with a committed annual plan, which includes free included storage up to a quota that scales with the size of the purchased baseline slot reservation
explain: BigQuery's long-term storage pricing is automatic: tables/partitions not modified for 90 days are charged at half price ($0.01/GB/month vs $0.02/GB/month). For 1 PB of mostly-inactive data, this saves ~$10,000/month. No manual archival required.
concept: BigQuery long-term storage pricing, 90-day threshold, automatic cost reduction

q: A data engineering team needs Cloud Spanner to handle a sudden 10x traffic spike. What scaling action is fastest?
opts: Add more Cloud Spanner nodes manually via the console or gcloud — provisioning takes 10-15 minutes to take effect, and the application must wait for capacity before the spike can be absorbed
Spanner autoscaling automatically adjusts node count (processing units) within configured bounds in minutes with no manual intervention
Create a second Cloud Spanner instance in the same region and distribute traffic across both instances at the application layer using a client-side sharding scheme keyed on tenant_id
Scale up the underlying database machine type via an ALTER DATABASE statement to a higher tier, since Spanner exposes a machine_type knob analogous to Cloud SQL instance tier upgrades
explain: Cloud Spanner supports autoscaling: configure min/max processing units; Spanner automatically scales within bounds. This is faster than manual node addition. For immediate burst handling, ensure the max is set high enough before the spike. Spanner also handles traffic spikes gracefully within its provisioned capacity.
concept: Cloud Spanner autoscaling, processing units, traffic spike handling

q: You need to store genomic data (billions of VCF records) with complex queries (gene_id, chromosome, position range). Which storage option supports this best?
opts: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with native range partitioning on the chromosome column and a B-tree index on (gene_id, position), suitable for moderate row counts but not for billions of VCF records
BigQuery with partitioning on chromosome and clustering on gene_id — enables efficient range scans on position with sub-minute query times
Cloud Bigtable with a row key of chromosome#position designed for range scans and a column family per analysis pipeline, providing low-latency point reads at the cost of analytic SQL support
Firestore with genomic-specific compound indexes on (chromosome, gene_id, position) and document-per-VCF storage, leveraging Firestore's automatic indexing for compound equality and range filters
explain: BigQuery is ideal for genomic analytics: partition by chromosome (ranges 1-22 + X, Y, MT), cluster by gene_id and position. Complex analytical SQL queries (window functions, joins with gene annotations) run at petabyte scale. BigQuery also integrates with Vertex AI for ML on genomic features.
concept: BigQuery genomics, partitioning and clustering for range queries, scientific analytics

q: What is a Cloud Spanner 'split' and why does it matter for performance?
opts: A split is a manual operation invoked via DDL to divide a large Cloud Spanner table into smaller physical tables, requiring the DBA to define split boundaries and migrate rows accordingly

A split is how Spanner automatically divides data across servers (SpanNodes) for horizontal scaling. Poor row key design (sequential IDs) can cause hotspots where new rows always land on the same split. Use UUIDs or hashed keys to distribute splits evenly
A split creates a read-only replica of a Spanner database for geographic distribution, enabling reads from a secondary region without acquiring a global quorum for read-only transactions
A split is a Cloud Spanner backup copy stored in a different region for disaster recovery, automatically created by Spanner whenever a database exceeds a configured size threshold
explain: Spanner automatically splits data across servers to distribute load. Sequential row keys (auto-increment IDs) concentrate writes on the 'last' split — creating hotspots. Use random/UUIDs or bit-reversed sequence keys to ensure new rows land on different splits evenly.
concept: Cloud Spanner splits, hotspot prevention, key design, horizontal distribution

q: You need to configure a BigQuery reservation for a batch ETL workload and a separate reservation for interactive analytics, ensuring ETL jobs don't starve analytics users of slots. How do you configure this?
opts: Create a single BigQuery Reservation with HIGHEST priority assigned to analytics queries and rely on BigQuery's internal slot scheduler to deprioritize ETL jobs when interactive load spikes

Create two separate BigQuery Reservations (ETL_RESERVATION and ANALYTICS_RESERVATION); create Assignments for each project/folder; configure slot counts appropriately — each workload gets isolated slots
Use BigQuery on-demand pricing for the interactive analytics workload and a dedicated reservation for the ETL workload, expecting on-demand queries to bypass the reservation and run independently

Set the job priority to INTERACTIVE for the analytics queries and BATCH for the ETL queries — BigQuery's scheduler will automatically prioritize INTERACTIVE jobs over BATCH jobs in any contention window
explain: BigQuery Reservations provide slot isolation. Create ETL_RESERVATION with dedicated slots assigned to ETL projects/folders; create ANALYTICS_RESERVATION with slots assigned to analytics projects. Each workload uses only its reserved slots — neither can starve the other. Idle slots can optionally be shared via reservation idle slot sharing.
concept: BigQuery Reservations, workload isolation, slot assignments, multi-workload management

q: A Bigtable instance has 3 clusters for replication (us-central1, europe-west1, asia-east1). A write arrives at the us-central1 cluster. When is it available in europe-west1?
opts: Immediately — Bigtable uses synchronous replication that requires a write to be committed at all clusters before the originating client receives an acknowledgement back from the API
Within a few seconds — Bigtable replication is asynchronous; writes replicate in the background with typical latency of seconds. Reads from europe-west1 may serve stale data briefly
Within 1 hour — Bigtable replication batches changes hourly and pushes the accumulated change log to peer clusters via a background replication job that runs on a fixed cadence
Within 24 hours — Bigtable replication runs as a daily batch process that mirrors changes between clusters once per day to minimize cross-region network egress costs at scale
explain: Bigtable replication is asynchronous. Writes committed at one cluster replicate to others with typically seconds of lag (similar to Google's global network replication speed). Applications that require zero replication lag must use single-cluster routing and accept the geographic latency tradeoff.
concept: Bigtable replication, asynchronous replication, replication lag, multi-cluster

q: You need to efficiently store and query a 100 TB financial dataset where queries frequently join a 100 TB transactions table with a 10 GB accounts table. What BigQuery design minimizes query time?
opts: Store both tables separately as normalized BigQuery tables and let BigQuery's distributed broadcast-join planner handle performance, since the 10 GB accounts table is small enough to be broadcast
Denormalize: JOIN accounts into transactions at ingestion time, creating a single wide table. Alternatively, use a materialized view that pre-joins frequently used combinations
Use Cloud Bigtable to store the transactions table for low-latency point reads and BigQuery to store the accounts table, joining them at query time through a Dataflow streaming pipeline
Create a separate BigQuery table per account, each containing only that account's transactions, and query the union of relevant per-account tables on demand via a UNION ALL pattern
explain: For a 100 TB × 10 GB join executed frequently: pre-join at ingestion (denormalize the 10 GB accounts into transactions) creates a single wide table eliminating the join overhead entirely. Alternatively, materialize the joined result. 100 TB joins are expensive — pre-computation amortizes the cost.
concept: BigQuery denormalization, pre-joining, large table join optimization, materialized views

q: What is Cloud SQL's connection limit and how do you handle connection exhaustion in a high-concurrency application?
opts: Cloud SQL has unlimited concurrent connections by default and any connection errors observed by the client are caused by application-side socket leaks rather than instance-level limits

Cloud SQL has per-instance connection limits (varies by instance tier: ~4,000 for large instances). Connection pooling via Cloud SQL Proxy or PgBouncer prevents connection exhaustion by sharing a smaller pool of database connections across many application threads
Switch to Cloud Spanner instead of Cloud SQL because Spanner imposes no concurrent connection limit and scales transparently with the application's outbound connection count
Raise the Cloud SQL connection ceiling by issuing an ALTER SYSTEM SET max_connections statement directly against the PostgreSQL instance, then restarting the instance for the change to take effect
explain: Cloud SQL connection limits: a connections-per-second limit and a maximum concurrent connections limit (varies by tier). High-concurrency apps exhaust connections without pooling. Cloud SQL Auth Proxy with PgBouncer (for PostgreSQL) or ProxySQL (for MySQL) implement connection pooling — many app threads share fewer DB connections.
concept: Cloud SQL connection limits, connection pooling, PgBouncer, Cloud SQL Auth Proxy

q: You need to implement a data lineage system that automatically captures how BigQuery tables are created from upstream sources. Which GCP service provides this natively?
opts: Cloud Audit Logs combined with a manual lineage reconstruction pipeline that parses BigQuery jobCompleted log entries and extracts source/destination relationships from query SQL text
Dataplex Lineage API — automatically captures lineage events from BigQuery DML/DDL operations, Dataflow jobs, and Data Fusion pipelines without instrumentation
BigQuery INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT queried on a recurring basis with a custom SQL parser that extracts referenced tables from each job's submitted query text and stores edges in a graph
Apache Atlas deployed on a Dataproc cluster with custom Hive and BigQuery hooks that emit lineage events to the Atlas metadata catalog whenever a downstream consumer processes a dataset
explain: Dataplex Lineage API auto-captures lineage: when a BigQuery CREATE TABLE AS SELECT runs, Dataplex records the source tables and destination. Dataflow and Data Fusion jobs also emit lineage events automatically. The resulting lineage graph shows upstream and downstream dependencies for any data asset.
concept: Dataplex Lineage API, automatic lineage capture, BigQuery lineage

q: Which Cloud Storage feature prevents objects from being deleted or overwritten for a specified retention period, useful for regulatory compliance?
opts: Cloud Storage Object Versioning, which keeps all previous versions of an object whenever it is overwritten or deleted, allowing administrators to restore any previous state on demand
Cloud Storage Retention Policies on buckets — objects cannot be deleted until the policy duration expires. Retention Lock makes the policy itself irremovable
Cloud Storage Lifecycle Management rules configured to block delete operations based on object age and prefix conditions, enforcing a hold against any user-initiated delete request
IAM deny policies attached to the bucket that prevent deletion by all users including project owners and administrators for the configured retention duration of the regulatory window
explain: Cloud Storage Retention Policies: set a minimum retention duration on a bucket. Objects cannot be deleted or overwritten until the policy duration expires from the creation/modification time. Locking the policy makes it permanent and cannot be shortened — required for SEC Rule 17a-4 and similar regulations.
concept: Cloud Storage retention policies, compliance, retention lock, WORM storage

q: A BigQuery table has JSON data stored as STRING columns. Queries need to extract values using JSON_VALUE() functions. What are the performance implications?
opts: JSON_VALUE() applied to STRING columns performs identically to direct access on natively typed columns because BigQuery's planner detects JSON-shaped strings and applies the same column scan optimizations
JSON_VALUE() on STRING columns requires parsing the JSON string at query time for every row scanned — significantly slower and more CPU-intensive than native typed columns or the BigQuery JSON type
BigQuery caches JSON parsing results internally after the first query, so the first run against a STRING-backed JSON column is slow but every subsequent query of any kind is served from cache
STRING columns containing JSON outperform STRUCT columns because they avoid the per-row schema metadata overhead that STRUCT introduces during columnar block decoding at scan time
explain: JSON stored as STRING requires runtime parsing via JSON_VALUE(). For large tables, this CPU overhead is significant. Better alternatives: (1) use BigQuery's native JSON type (optimized internal storage with lazy parsing); (2) use STRUCT columns for known schemas; (3) parse at ingestion time into typed columns.
concept: BigQuery JSON performance, JSON_VALUE overhead, schema design

q: You need to create a BigQuery table that stores user events. The table will be queried 90% of the time by user_id and event_type. You also need to occasionally run full table scans. Which design is optimal?
opts: No partitioning and no clustering, since full table scans must read every block regardless of layout and adding clustering would only add overhead without benefit to either workload pattern
Partition by _PARTITIONTIME (ingestion time) and cluster by user_id, event_type. Queries filtering by user_id and event_type eliminate blocks; full scans are unaffected by clustering overhead
Cluster by user_id and event_type without partitioning, relying on cluster-block elimination alone to optimize the 90% of user-filtered queries since the table will be scanned in full periodically
Partition by user_id directly to enable per-user partition pruning, accepting that this design will create up to one BigQuery partition per distinct user_id value at ingestion time
explain: Ingestion-time partitioning limits the total data scanned by date for most queries. Clustering by user_id, event_type enables block elimination for the 90% of user_id/event_type queries within each partition. Full table scans are not penalized by clustering — they read all blocks regardless. This is the optimal combination.
concept: BigQuery partitioning and clustering combination, block elimination, full scan behavior

q: You need to implement cross-region disaster recovery for a Bigtable production cluster. A failure in us-central1 should automatically failover reads to europe-west1. How do you configure this?
opts: Manually update the application's DNS records to point at the europe-west1 Bigtable cluster endpoint after detecting a failure in us-central1, then redeploy the application to pick up the new target

Configure a multi-cluster Bigtable instance with clusters in us-central1 and europe-west1; use an app profile with multi-cluster routing (any-cluster). On failure, Bigtable automatically routes requests to the available cluster
Enable Bigtable cross-region replication and configure a Cloud Load Balancing backend service in front of the two cluster endpoints, using HTTP(S) health checks to drive automatic regional failover
Switch from Bigtable to Cloud Spanner for the workload because Bigtable does not support automatic failover between clusters and Spanner is the only GCP database with native multi-region failover
explain: Bigtable multi-cluster routing: create two clusters (us-central1 + europe-west1) in a single Bigtable instance; configure an app profile with routing_policy=multi-cluster-use-any. If us-central1 becomes unavailable, Bigtable automatically routes to europe-west1 — no application changes needed.
concept: Bigtable multi-cluster routing, automatic failover, disaster recovery, app profiles

q: What is the difference between BigQuery's ARRAY and STRUCT types and when would you use each?
opts: ARRAY and STRUCT are functionally identical complex types in BigQuery — both store multiple values inside a single column, and the choice between them is purely a question of stylistic preference

ARRAY stores an ordered list of same-type values (e.g., ARRAY<STRING>). STRUCT stores named fields of different types (like a row within a row). Use ARRAY for repeated values of one type; use STRUCT for grouping related fields; use ARRAY<STRUCT<...>> for repeated nested records
ARRAY is restricted to storing numeric primitive types such as INT64 and FLOAT64 while STRUCT is the only complex type that supports string, date, and timestamp values inside the same field grouping
STRUCT is the BigQuery replacement for traditional row-level normalization while ARRAY is the recommended replacement for table-to-table joins, eliminating the need for relational schema design
explain: BigQuery's nested data model: ARRAY<T> is a repeated field of a single type (e.g., list of tags). STRUCT<field1 TYPE1, field2 TYPE2> nests multiple named fields. ARRAY<STRUCT<...>> represents repeated nested records (e.g., an array of order line items). These enable storing denormalized hierarchical data efficiently.
concept: BigQuery ARRAY and STRUCT, nested data model, denormalization

q: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL needs to handle geospatial queries (ST_Within, ST_Distance). Which extension provides this capability?
opts: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL includes geospatial query support natively through a built-in GEOMETRY column type that exposes ST_Within and ST_Distance functions without any extension being enabled
PostGIS extension — enable it with CREATE EXTENSION postgis; provides geometry/geography types and spatial functions. Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL fully supports PostGIS
Cloud SQL Geospatial API, a managed GCP service exposed as a separate endpoint for executing spatial queries against tables stored in any Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL instance in the project
BigQuery GIS functions invoked via a federated query from Cloud SQL into BigQuery, since Cloud SQL itself cannot evaluate ST_Within or ST_Distance and must delegate spatial work to BigQuery
explain: PostGIS is the standard PostgreSQL geospatial extension. Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL supports PostGIS — enable it via CREATE EXTENSION postgis. This provides: geometry/geography data types, spatial indexes (GiST), and spatial functions (ST_Within, ST_Distance, ST_Intersection) required for geospatial queries.
concept: Cloud SQL PostGIS, geospatial queries, PostgreSQL extensions

q: A BigQuery project is shared by 50 teams. One team runs a rogue query that scans the entire 100 TB dataset daily, causing slot contention. How do you prevent this?
opts: Grant the offending team only the roles/bigquery.dataViewer role at the dataset level, since read-only access prevents the team from consuming any slot capacity during query execution
Set a maximum bytes billed per query on that team's project (service account); add BigQuery project-level quotas for max bytes per day. Use BigQuery Reservations to isolate slot pools per team
Revoke the team's BigQuery access entirely until they formally agree in writing to follow query optimization best practices, then reinstate dataset-level permissions after a training session
Use BigQuery authorized views to limit the data the team can see by exposing only a filtered subset of rows and columns, with the expectation that this also limits their slot consumption
explain: Two controls: (1) maximum bytes billed per query prevents individual runaway queries; (2) per-project daily byte quotas cap total consumption. BigQuery Reservations provide slot isolation — the rogue team's Reservation limits their slots without affecting other teams.
concept: BigQuery cost controls, bytes billed limits, per-project quotas, Reservations isolation

q: You need to implement a BigQuery table clone for development testing without incurring the full storage cost of a copy. Which BigQuery feature provides this?
opts: BigQuery table copy via the bq cp command, which creates a full physical copy of the source table in the destination dataset with storage billed at the standard active storage rate per byte
BigQuery table clone (CREATE TABLE ... CLONE) — creates a zero-storage-cost clone using copy-on-write semantics; only changed rows incur additional storage
BigQuery table snapshot via CREATE SNAPSHOT TABLE, which creates a read-only point-in-time copy of the source table where unchanged data is shared but writes are not permitted on the snapshot
BigQuery materialized view that precomputes and stores a query result against the source table, refreshing incrementally as the base table changes and behaving like a derived dev copy
explain: BigQuery table clones use copy-on-write: CREATE TABLE dev.events CLONE prod.events creates a logical copy that references the original data. Storage cost is zero until rows are modified in the clone. Only changed/added rows in the clone incur storage cost — perfect for dev/test without the 100% storage overhead of a full copy.
concept: BigQuery table clone, copy-on-write, zero-storage-cost development tables

q: Firestore's maximum document size is 1 MB. A design stores large text blobs (up to 10 MB) in documents. How should you redesign this?
opts: Apply GZIP compression to the large text blobs at the application layer so each compressed payload fits inside the 1 MB Firestore document limit, then decompress on the read side at runtime
Store the large blobs in Cloud Storage; store the GCS URI in the Firestore document as a reference. Firestore documents contain only metadata and the pointer to the blob
Split each oversized document into multiple sub-documents under a subcollection so that each child sub-document stays under the 1 MB limit, then reassemble them on read at the client
Switch from Firestore to Cloud Spanner for the workload because Spanner has no per-row payload size limit and can store the entire 10 MB blob inline within a single relational row
explain: The Firestore pattern for large blobs: store metadata (title, author, timestamps, tags) in Firestore for fast querying; store the actual blob (PDF, large text, media) in Cloud Storage. The Firestore document includes the GCS URI. Applications fetch metadata from Firestore and the blob from GCS separately.
concept: Firestore document limits, blob storage pattern, Firestore + Cloud Storage

q: You need to implement a BigQuery access pattern where different users see different versions of the same data (some see real values, some see masked values, some see NULL). Which feature set implements this with a single underlying table?
opts: Create three separate BigQuery tables with different data variants for each user group and grant each group dataViewer access only on the table that corresponds to their masking requirement

BigQuery Dynamic Data Masking: create masking rules (NULL_MASK, DEFAULT_MASK, SHA256_MASK, etc.) on policy-tagged columns; bind different masking rules to different IAM principals — same table, different views depending on user
Use three BigQuery authorized views, each with a CASE WHEN SESSION_USER() expression that branches the column projection based on the current authenticated user identity at query time
Use BigQuery Row Access Policies with different FILTER USING conditions per user group, expecting the row filter to also mask out column values based on the requesting principal's group membership
explain: Dynamic Data Masking allows multiple masking rules on the same column: Admin group → unmasked values; Analyst group → SHA256 hash; External group → NULL. All groups query the same table — BigQuery applies the appropriate mask at query time based on the user's group membership.
concept: BigQuery Dynamic Data Masking, multiple masking rules, role-based data views

q: What is the recommended row key design for a Bigtable table storing user clickstream events where you need to: (1) retrieve all events for a user, (2) retrieve the last N events for a user, and (3) avoid hotspots?
opts: user_id#event_id with sequential numeric event_ids, providing simple per-user prefix scans but causing the trailing sequential portion to concentrate new writes on the last tablet under high load
Hashed_user_id (8 chars)#reverse_timestamp#event_id — hash prefix distributes writes across tablets; reverse timestamp sorts recent events first within each user
timestamp#user_id as the primary key ordering, enabling time-based range scans across all users globally but funneling all current writes onto the most recent timestamp tablet at any moment
event_id alone as a fully random UUID, providing excellent write distribution and no hotspots but eliminating any ability to do efficient per-user prefix range scans on the row key
explain: This design achieves all three requirements: (1) hashed_user_id prefix scatters writes across tablets preventing hotspots for any single user; (2) all events for a user share the same hashed prefix enabling prefix scans; (3) reverse_timestamp within the prefix sorts recent events first — limit N returns the N most recent events.
concept: Bigtable row key design, multi-criteria optimization, hash prefix, reversed timestamp

q: BigQuery supports which of the following transaction isolation levels for DML operations?
opts: Read Uncommitted — the fastest option available but can read dirty data from in-flight transactions that have not yet committed their changes to the underlying table storage
Serializable isolation for all DML operations, which acquires range locks on the affected rows and guarantees full serial ordering of concurrent DML statements against any table
Snapshot isolation — DML reads a consistent snapshot at statement start; concurrent DML is detected at commit and retried automatically
No isolation level is provided for DML — concurrent UPDATE and DELETE statements against the same table may produce undefined or non-deterministic results in the persisted storage
explain: BigQuery DML uses snapshot isolation: each statement reads data from a consistent snapshot taken at statement start. Concurrent DML statements can run simultaneously; conflicts are detected at commit time and BigQuery retries the conflicting statement automatically (up to 5 times).
concept: BigQuery DML isolation, snapshot isolation, concurrent DML, ACID

q: You need to store time-series metrics for 10,000 servers, each emitting 100 metrics per second. Queries need: (1) last 5 minutes of any metric for one server, (2) aggregate across all servers for a specific metric. Which Bigtable row key design serves both patterns?
opts: server_id#metric_name#timestamp serves the per-server query well, but cross-server metric aggregation must scan the entire table because the metric_name component is not at the leading position
metric_name#server_id#reverse_timestamp — serves both: metric aggregation via metric_name prefix scan; per-server metric via metric_name#server_id prefix; reverse timestamp gives recency
timestamp#server_id#metric_name supports time-windowed scans across all servers but creates a sustained write hotspot at the leading edge of time, since all new writes share the latest timestamp
server_id#reverse_timestamp#metric_name serves per-server queries efficiently but forces metric aggregation queries to perform a full table scan because metric_name is not a leading prefix
explain: metric_name#server_id#reverse_timestamp serves both access patterns: (1) scan prefix metric_name#server_id# with row limit 300 for last 5 minutes (300 = 5 min × 60 sec × 1 reading/sec per metric); (2) scan prefix metric_name# for cross-server aggregation. Reverse timestamp avoids hotspots on current time.
concept: Bigtable multi-pattern row key design, time-series metrics, cross-server aggregation

q: A Cloud Storage bucket is accessed from both GCP and on-premises. On-premises access is slow. What can improve performance?
opts: Enable Cloud CDN in front of the Cloud Storage bucket, which caches frequently accessed objects in Google edge POPs near the on-premises location and serves them with low TTL latency
Convert the bucket to a Cloud Storage multi-region configuration so that object replicas are automatically placed in the region geographically closest to the on-premises data center
