Deploy a storage proxy on-premises using Cloud Storage FUSE or a Cloud Storage client library with transfer optimization; also use Cloud Interconnect for dedicated bandwidth instead of public internet
Migrate the data out of Cloud Storage and into Cloud SQL because Cloud SQL has lower latency for on-premises access patterns and exposes a TCP endpoint that can be reached over VPN
explain: Cloud Storage over public internet has variable latency. Cloud Interconnect provides dedicated, low-latency, high-bandwidth connectivity to GCP from on-premises. Additionally, use the Cloud Storage client library's parallel composite uploads/downloads and JSON API resumable uploads to maximize throughput.
concept: Cloud Storage on-premises access, Cloud Interconnect, transfer optimization

q: You need to implement a system where BigQuery table access is automatically revoked after 30 days of inactivity. What is the mechanism?
opts: BigQuery automatically revokes IAM bindings after 30 days of dataset inactivity by default through a built-in cleanup process that runs as part of the BigQuery managed identity lifecycle

Use IAM Conditions with a date expression (request.time < timestamp('2024-03-01')) to create time-bound IAM bindings; or implement a Cloud Function that checks last query date from INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS and revokes access if > 30 days
Use BigQuery audit logs to generate a monthly 30-day access report listing principals who have not queried the dataset, and manually revoke IAM bindings for the listed inactive principals
Use Dataplex IAM policies on the underlying lake asset with time-based access conditions on the resource hierarchy that automatically expire dataset-level bindings on the configured cadence
explain: GCP IAM supports time-based conditions: bind roles with condition request.time < timestamp('date'). For dynamic 30-day windows, implement a Cloud Scheduler + Cloud Function that checks INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS per user for query activity in the last 30 days and removes IAM bindings for inactive users.
concept: IAM Conditions, time-based access, automated access revocation, INFORMATION_SCHEMA

q: What is the purpose of BigQuery's 'require_partition_filter' table option?
opts: It requires BigQuery to use a partition filter for all queries on the table — queries without a partition filter in the WHERE clause are rejected with an error
It enables automatic partition pruning on all queries against the table by injecting an implicit partition filter at query plan time based on observed query patterns over recent history
It requires all rows inserted into the table to specify an explicit partition value through the _PARTITIONTIME pseudo-column rather than relying on ingestion-time partition assignment
It prevents any single query against the table from referencing more than one partition at a time, forcing per-partition iteration when multi-partition results are needed by the application
explain: Setting require_partition_filter=TRUE on a BigQuery partitioned table forces all queries to include a filter on the partitioned column. Queries without the filter are rejected: 'Error: Cannot query over table without a filter that can be used for partition elimination.' This prevents accidental full table scans on large partitioned tables.
concept: BigQuery require_partition_filter, query guardrails, partition safety

q: A BigQuery dataset needs to be shared with users who are not in your GCP organization. What is the correct sharing mechanism?
opts: Cross-organization sharing in BigQuery is blocked by default org policy and there is no IAM mechanism available to grant access to identities outside the source GCP organization boundary

Grant IAM roles directly to external users' Google accounts (gmail.com or Workspace accounts) on the BigQuery dataset — BigQuery IAM supports non-org identities; or use Analytics Hub for structured data exchange
Create a service account dedicated to external collaboration and securely share the JSON key file with the external user, who then authenticates as that service account to query the dataset
External users must first create a new GCP project within your organization and accept an invitation, after which they can be granted BigQuery roles on the shared dataset as internal identities
explain: BigQuery IAM supports granting access to any Google account (including @gmail.com accounts outside your organization). Grant roles/bigquery.dataViewer on the specific dataset to the external user's email address. Analytics Hub provides a more structured data exchange mechanism with subscription management.
concept: BigQuery cross-organization sharing, external IAM, Analytics Hub

q: Explain the difference between Bigtable's 'Read Rows' and 'Sample Row Keys' API calls and when each is used.
opts: Read Rows and Sample Row Keys are aliases for the same underlying API call within the Bigtable client library and can be used interchangeably to retrieve actual row data from the cluster

Read Rows retrieves actual row data (all cells or specified cell sets). Sample Row Keys returns approximate boundary keys that divide the table into roughly equal segments — used by parallel job frameworks (Dataflow BigtableIO) to split scans across multiple workers without reading data
Sample Row Keys is a streaming low-latency API for continuous reads while Read Rows is a batch-mode API designed only for one-shot retrieval of historical data with higher tail latency
Read Rows is optimized for small tables under 1 TB while Sample Row Keys is the only supported API for tables that exceed 1 TB of total stored data across all column families and rows
explain: SampleRowKeys is the split planning API: it returns ~1000 boundary keys per cluster node, enabling Dataflow/MapReduce to create parallel scan ranges. Each worker then calls ReadRows on its assigned key range. Without SampleRowKeys, parallel frameworks would have to scan sequentially.
concept: Bigtable SampleRowKeys, parallel scan planning, Dataflow BigtableIO

q: A BigQuery table is updated every hour via a scheduled query. Analysts notice that their materialized views over this table sometimes show stale data. Why?
opts: BigQuery materialized views do not refresh automatically and require an explicit CALL BQ.REFRESH_MATERIALIZED_VIEW statement to be executed before each downstream analytical query

BigQuery materialized views have a maximum staleness of 30 minutes by default. If the base table changes within the last refresh cycle, the materialized view serves slightly stale data. Queries can use the max_staleness option or trigger manual refresh
BigQuery materialized views always display data from the previous calendar day's table state because the refresh job runs once per 24 hours on the BigQuery managed maintenance window
BigQuery materialized views are never stale because they are refreshed synchronously with every commit on the base table through an internal change data capture mechanism in the storage engine
explain: BigQuery materialized views refresh incrementally when queried (if the base table changed). The default max staleness is 30 minutes — if you query within 30 minutes of a base table change, BigQuery serves the last cached result. Use OPTIONS(max_staleness=INTERVAL '1' MINUTE) for lower staleness tolerance.
concept: BigQuery materialized view staleness, refresh cycle, max_staleness option

q: You are designing a multi-region BigQuery architecture where the same analytical queries need to run in both the US and EU on locally stored copies of the data. What is the most operational approach?
opts: Write the data to two separate BigQuery datasets (one US, one EU) from the application pipeline; maintain identical schemas and rely on application-level coordination for ordering
Use BigQuery Cross-Region Replication (when available) or set up two separate pipeline sinks (US dataset + EU dataset) from a shared Pub/Sub topic; use Cloud Composer to ensure both are synchronized
Use a single BigQuery multi-region dataset (e.g., US multi-region) and rely on BigQuery to automatically route queries to the underlying nearest region without any application-level configuration
Use Datastream to continuously replicate BigQuery tables between the US and EU regions on a per-row change data capture stream that mirrors every DML statement applied on the source dataset
explain: For multi-region BigQuery with local data: write from a single ingest pipeline (Pub/Sub/Dataflow) to two separate regional datasets simultaneously. Cloud Composer orchestrates the dual writes and monitors freshness. Each region has its own dataset complying with data residency requirements.
concept: BigQuery multi-region architecture, dual-write pattern, data residency

q: What is the purpose of Cloud Storage's 'object versioning' feature and what are its cost implications?
opts: Object versioning automatically compresses prior object versions using gzip to reduce the storage footprint of non-current versions, with cost savings increasing as the version count grows

Object versioning retains previous versions of objects when they are overwritten or deleted. Each previous version is stored as a non-current version and billed at full storage rates. Use lifecycle rules (condition: isLive=false, action: Delete after N days) to control versioning costs
Object versioning is included at no additional cost — previous versions of objects are stored without any per-byte storage charges as long as the bucket has versioning enabled at any point
Object versioning stores only the binary delta between successive versions of an object rather than the full version content, minimizing the additional storage cost incurred per overwrite
explain: Cloud Storage versioning stores complete copies of each previous version — not diffs. Each non-current version is billed at full storage rates. Without lifecycle rules to delete old versions, costs can grow unboundedly. Best practice: enable versioning + lifecycle rule to delete non-current versions after N days.
concept: Cloud Storage object versioning, non-current versions, versioning cost management

q: A Cloud Spanner table has a hotspot on a frequently-read row (the 'global settings' row accessed by all users). How do you resolve this read hotspot?
opts: Increase the Cloud Spanner node count so that more nodes are available to handle reads against the hot row, expecting Spanner's internal load balancer to spread reads of the row across nodes

Application-level caching (Cloud Memorystore Redis, in-process cache): cache the global settings row in the application layer with a TTL; only read from Spanner on cache miss. The hot row reads become cache hits — Spanner load drops dramatically
Create a dedicated Cloud Spanner read-only replica for the global settings table and route all read traffic for that row to the replica using bounded staleness reads on the replica endpoint
Partition the global settings table along the primary key so that the hotspot row is split across multiple Spanner splits, distributing the read load across multiple underlying SpanNodes
explain: A single frequently-read Spanner row creates a read hotspot on one split. Spanner cannot distribute reads of a single row across nodes. Solution: application-level caching (Memorystore Redis) with appropriate TTL. The read-heavy row is served from cache; Spanner only serves cache misses (infrequent).
concept: Cloud Spanner read hotspot, application caching, Memorystore Redis, cache-aside pattern

q: You need to store and query 50 billion GPS coordinates (latitude, longitude, timestamp, device_id) with queries: (1) find all coordinates for device_id in a time range; (2) find all coordinates in a geographic bounding box. Which storage design handles both queries?
opts: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with PostGIS geospatial indexing handles bounding box queries effectively but does not scale to 50 billion rows of GPS coordinate data within a single managed instance
BigQuery with a device_id + timestamp partition/cluster for query (1); use BigQuery GIS functions (ST_Within, ST_DWithin) for geographic queries on the same table
Bigtable with a device_id#timestamp row key handles per-device range scans for query (1); geographic queries require building a secondary Bigtable table keyed on a geohash for bounding box lookups
Both B and C; BigQuery for analytics, Bigtable for real-time with appropriate row key design
explain: Optimal design depends on use case: BigQuery (partitioned by date, clustered by device_id) handles both analytical queries via SQL — time range and ST_Within for geographic queries — but isn't real-time. Bigtable handles real-time per-device queries. For a full system, BigQuery handles analytics; Bigtable handles real-time serving.
concept: GPS data storage, BigQuery GIS, Bigtable time-series, multi-system architecture

q: A BigQuery dataset is in the EU multi-region. A Dataflow job in us-central1 writes to this dataset. Is this a problem?
opts: No — the Dataflow job's worker region and the destination BigQuery dataset region do not need to match because BigQuery accepts inserts from any GCP region via its global API endpoint
Yes — data transfer from us-central1 Dataflow to EU BigQuery incurs cross-region network egress charges and may violate EU data residency requirements since data traverses the US
No — Google encrypts all data in transit between regions using TLS and Google-managed keys so the configuration is automatically compliant with EU data residency and GDPR requirements
Yes — Dataflow cannot write to a BigQuery dataset that is located in a different region from the worker pool and the job will fail with a region-mismatch error at the BigQueryIO sink
explain: Cross-region data transfer: Dataflow workers in us-central1 process data in the US and write to EU BigQuery. This means data temporarily resides in the US (Dataflow workers) before moving to EU — potentially violating EU data residency requirements. Also incurs cross-region egress charges. Solution: deploy Dataflow in europe-west1 or europe-west4.
concept: Cross-region data processing, data residency compliance, Dataflow region selection

q: What is the Bigtable garbage collection (GC) policy and how does it help manage storage?
opts: The GC policy automatically rebalances row data across Bigtable nodes by detecting tablet-level imbalance and migrating rows toward less-utilized nodes during background maintenance windows

GC policies define retention rules for cell versions per column family: maxVersions (keep only N most recent versions) and maxAge (delete versions older than N days). Bigtable applies GC asynchronously in the background — reducing storage by removing obsolete cell versions
The GC policy is a billing-tier feature that automatically transitions cold Bigtable data to Cloud Storage Archive after a configurable inactivity threshold, lowering the per-GB storage cost
The GC policy applies retention enforcement only at the level of entire rows rather than individual cell versions, so each policy decision either keeps or deletes the row in its entirety
explain: Bigtable GC policies are defined per column family: maxVersions keeps the N most recent timestamps for each cell; maxAge deletes cell versions older than a specified duration. These can be combined with union/intersection logic. Without GC policies, every write creates a new version and storage grows unboundedly.
concept: Bigtable GC policy, cell versions, storage management, maxVersions, maxAge

q: You need to implement BigQuery table-level access where Project A can query tables in Project B's dataset. How do you configure this?
opts: Cross-project BigQuery access is only supported when both source and destination projects are attached to the same shared VPC network, with the BigQuery API enabled in both projects

Grant a role (e.g., roles/bigquery.dataViewer) to a Project A service account on the specific Project B dataset; queries from Project A use the TABLE syntax: project_b.dataset.table — no VPC configuration needed
Use BigQuery Data Transfer Service to schedule a recurring copy of Project B's dataset into Project A, since direct cross-project queries are not permitted across organization boundaries
Create a VPC peering connection between Project A's and Project B's networks so the BigQuery API can route cross-project read traffic privately through the peered network attachment
explain: BigQuery IAM is resource-based and project-agnostic. Grant the Project A service account roles/bigquery.dataViewer on the Project B dataset. Project A queries use fully qualified table names (project_b.dataset.table) in SQL. No VPC or network configuration required.
concept: BigQuery cross-project access, resource-based IAM, fully qualified table names

q: A Cloud Storage Standard bucket receives 10,000 writes/second and 100,000 reads/second. You observe increasing read latency as operations grow. What is likely causing this and how do you fix it?
opts: Cloud Storage caps total bucket operations at 1,000 per second across all clients regardless of bucket configuration, so the only remedy is to contact GCP support and request a manual quota increase

Cloud Storage has per-prefix QPS limits (~1,000 writes/sec, ~5,000 reads/sec per prefix). At your scale, you're hitting prefix hotspots. Fix: randomize object name prefixes (hash-prefix or date-based sharding) to distribute operations across multiple prefixes
Upgrade the bucket to a Cloud Storage multi-region configuration so that read traffic is spread across multiple underlying regional replicas, raising the effective per-bucket QPS ceiling
Enable Cloud Storage's parallel composite upload feature so that each large object is uploaded as multiple parallel chunks, reducing the per-write API call overhead on the bucket frontend
explain: Cloud Storage has per-prefix throughput limits. Objects with the same prefix (e.g., all starting with 'data/') hit limits on that prefix. Fix: add a random hash prefix (e.g., first 4 chars of MD5 of object name as prefix) to distribute objects across multiple prefixes, each with independent throughput limits.
concept: Cloud Storage QPS limits, prefix hotspots, object name distribution
